goal: Add 13 Tier-1 (trivial) utilities to lentils: uname, arch, hostid, logname, tty, whoami, uptime, printenv, nproc, users, groups, id, seq

milestones.title: Proc-file readers (uname, arch, hostid, uptime)
milestones.objective: Implement 4 utilities that read a single value from /proc filesystem. No FFI needed — use Lean's IO.FS.readFile.

features.title: uname — print system information
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Uname/Logic.lean with a structure for uname info (sysname, nodename, release, version, machine). Create Lentils/Uname/Uname.lean. On Linux, read from /proc/sys/kernel/{ostype, hostname, osrelease, version}. Fall back to environment variables (HOSTNAME, etc). Format like POSIX: 'Linux hostname 6.2.0-... #1 SMP ... x86_64' without flags, or support -a, -s, -n, -r, -v, -m flags. Minimal: just print uname -a output. Use parseArgs with combined flag support (the pattern from Grep).

features.title: arch — print machine architecture
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Arch/Logic.lean and Lentils/Arch/Arch.lean. Read /proc/sys/kernel/arch or /proc/sys/kernel/machine. Print the machine architecture (x86_64, aarch64, etc.). No flags needed. If /proc unavailable, fall back to 'unknown'.

features.title: hostid — print numeric host identifier
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Hostid/Logic.lean and Lentils/Hostid/Hostid.lean. Read /proc/sys/kernel/hostid (4-byte hex). Print as 8-digit hex (e.g., '007f0100'). No flags needed.

features.title: uptime — print system uptime
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Uptime/Logic.lean and Lentils/Uptime/Uptime.lean. Create a pure function that formats uptime seconds into a human-readable string (e.g., '2 hours, 15 minutes'). Read /proc/uptime (first field = seconds as float). Parse the float. Format: 'X days, Y hours, Z minutes' for long uptimes, or just 'X min' for short ones. No flags needed.

milestones.title: Environment readers (logname, whoami, printenv, tty)
milestones.objective: Implement 4 utilities that read from environment variables or /proc. All can be done with Lean's built-in IO or /proc reads.

features.title: logname — print user's login name
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Logname/Logic.lean and Lentils/Logname/Logname.lean. Read from environment variable LOGNAME (POSIX standard), fall back to USER. Print the name followed by newline. Exit 0 on success, 1 if neither variable is set.

features.title: whoami — print effective user ID name
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Whoami/Logic.lean and Lentils/Whoami/Whoami.lean. Read /proc/self/status, find 'Uid:' line, take the effective UID (second field). Then read /etc/passwd to look up the username for that UID, OR read the NAME environment variable. For simplicity on Linux, read /proc/self/loginuid or use $USER. Print the username followed by newline.

features.title: printenv — print environment variables
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Printenv/Logic.lean and Lentils/Printenv/Printenv.lean. If arguments given, print each named variable (VAR=value) if it exists, exit 1 if any are missing. If no arguments... Lean's IO.getEnv can read specific variables but can't enumerate all. For a complete implementation, add a C FFI function to coreutils.c: 'lean_coreutils_environ()' that returns an array of strings from the global 'environ'. Then in the IO wrapper, call that FFI, parse each 'KEY=VALUE' pair, sort them, and print.

features.title: tty — print terminal file name
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Tty/Logic.lean and Lentils/Tty/Tty.lean. Readlink /proc/self/fd/0 to get the terminal device name. If it's not a terminal (e.g., pipe), print 'not a tty' and exit 1. If it is a tty, print the device path (e.g., '/dev/pts/0') and exit 0. Support --help. No flag parsing needed beyond --help check.
validation: bash -c 'if [ -t 0 ]; then .lake/build/bin/lentils tty 2>&1 | grep -q /dev; else .lake/build/bin/lentils tty 2>&1 | grep -q "not a tty"; fi'

milestones.title: System queries (nproc, users, groups, id)
milestones.objective: Implement 4 utilities that query system resources. nproc can read /proc/cpuinfo. users, groups, and id need /proc/self/status parsing.

features.title: nproc — print number of processing units
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Nproc/Logic.lean and Lentils/Nproc/Nproc.lean. Read /proc/cpuinfo and count lines matching 'processor\t*:'. Print the count. No flags needed (--ignore and --all are optional). Exit 0.

features.title: users — print logged-in user names
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Users/Logic.lean and Lentils/Users/Users.lean. Parse /var/run/utmp (binary format defined in <utmp.h>) to find active login sessions. This requires a C FFI function 'lean_coreutils_users()' in coreutils.c that calls getutxent() in a loop, extracts ut_user for entries with ut_type == USER_PROCESS, and returns them as a Lean list (Array String). Deduplicate and print space-separated. If /var/run/utmp is unreadable or empty, print nothing and exit 0.

features.title: groups — print group memberships
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Groups/Logic.lean and Lentils/Groups/Groups.lean. Read /proc/self/status, find 'Groups:' line, parse the space-separated GID list. Add a C FFI function 'lean_coreutils_getgroups()' in coreutils.c that calls getgroups() and returns the list of supplementary GIDs. Look up each GID in /etc/group for group names (add FFI getgrgid_r, or just print numeric GIDs). Print space-separated group names.

features.title: id — print user and group identity
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Id/Logic.lean and Lentils/Id/Id.lean. Read /proc/self/status for Uid/Gid lines (real, effective, saved, filesystem). Format like POSIX: 'uid=N(NAME) gid=N(NAME) groups=N(NAME),...'. Need C FFI for getpwuid_r (name lookup) and getgrgid_r, or parse /etc/passwd and /etc/group directly. Support flags: (none) for default, -u (effective UID), -g (effective GID), -G (supplementary groups), -n (print names instead of numbers), -r (real instead of effective). Use parseArgs with combined flag support.

milestones.title: seq — print sequence of numbers
milestones.objective: Implement seq, the only purely computational utility in Tier 1. No FFI or /proc needed.

features.title: seq — print sequence of numbers
features.instructions: Create Lentils/Seq/Logic.lean and Lentils/Seq/Seq.lean. seq takes 1-3 arguments: [first [increment]] last. Generate numbers from first to last, stepping by increment. Default first=1, increment=1. Print each number on its own line. Handle: seq LAST, seq FIRST LAST, seq FIRST INCREMENT LAST. Support floats with decimal formatting (trim trailing zeros, keep at least one decimal if input has decimals). Support -w flag (pad with leading zeros to equal width), -s STR (separator instead of newline), -f FORMAT (printf-style format string). Use parseArgs with combined flag support. Core logic is pure (no IO except printing).

notes: 9/13 done: arch, logname, whoami, nproc, uname, hostid, uptime, tty, seq (commits 4a8bc109, 9df3a112)
Remaining 4 need C FFI in coreutils.c: printenv (environ), users (getutxent), groups (getgroups), id (getuid/getpwuid)
Deprecations fixed: String.mk → String.ofList, Nat.toFloat → (UInt64.ofNat n).toFloat, custom Float parser for Lean 4.31
seq off-by-one fixed: maxSteps = floor(diff/inc) + 1 to include both endpoints
13 new utilities would bring lentils from 20 → 33 utilities total
Each utility needs: Logic.lean (pure), Applet.lean (IO wrapper), Name.lean (re-export), imports in Lentils.lean, dispatch+help in Main.lean
FFI additions needed in c/coreutils.c: environ, getutxent, getgroups, getpwuid_r, getgrgid_r
On non-Linux systems, /proc-based implementations will fail gracefully — TODO: add portable FFI fallbacks later
Follow existing patterns: True/Logic.lean for trivial, Pwd/Pwd.lean for IO wrapper, Main.lean for dispatch
